questions.prompt: Key benefit of layout.tsx over rendering shared UI in each page?

choices.text: Layouts persist across client-side navigations between sibling routes — they don't re-mount, don't re-fetch, and preserve state. Going from /dashboard to /dashboard/settings keeps sidebar state, form inputs, scroll position; only the page content swaps. Layouts compose via directory nesting. The root layout is required and must include `<html>` and `<body>`.

questions.explanation: Layouts persist across sibling navigations; state preserved.

questions.prompt: Pre-rendering 5,000 blog posts but want to add new ones without rebuilding entire site. Setup?

choices.text: Export `generateStaticParams()` returning all existing slugs; pair with `export const revalidate = 60` for ISR. Build time pre-renders 5,000 posts. With `dynamicParams = true` (default), requests for new slugs render on-demand and cache. Existing posts served from CDN; new ones generate at first hit then cache. Best of static + dynamic without rebuilding the whole site.

Dashboards with independent panels. Each slot has its own loading.tsx and error.tsx; data fetches in parallel; one slot's error doesn't break another's render; one slot's slow query doesn't block other panels. Combined with bookmarkable URL state, dramatically cleaner than building one giant dashboard page. The layout receives `children` plus named slot props.

questions.explanation: Dashboards with independent loading/error/data per panel.

questions.prompt: Bookmarkable modal for photo gallery (click → modal; direct link → full page)?

choices.text: Intercepted routes. Create `app/photos/[id]/page.tsx` for full-page view + `app/@modal/(..)photos/[id]/page.tsx` to intercept from `/photos`. Click from grid → modal opens, URL updates; grid stays rendered underneath. Direct link to /photos/123 → full page renders. Both bookmarkable. `router.back()` closes modal. The `(..)` syntax means 'intercept from one level up.'

questions.explanation: Intercepted routes (`(..)`) render one route in another's context.

questions.prompt: What's the right work for middleware?

choices.text: Fast cross-cutting checks: auth gating (cookie/JWT presence and validity), redirects and rewrites, A/B testing routing, geo-based routing, locale detection, security headers, rate limiting via HTTP-based KV stores. Middleware runs at edge on every matched request; heavy work or DB queries add latency to everything. Edge has constraints (no Node APIs, strict timeouts). Use middleware for unglamorous fast gating; save real work for pages/actions.

questions.explanation: Fast cross-cutting checks only; real work belongs in pages/actions.
